region: Bocas del Toro Archipelago, Caribbean Panama
desc: Bocas del Toro is a boat-shaped trip as much as a beach trip: Bocas Town on Isla Colon for bars and logistics, Bastimentos and Red Frog for jungle-edge sand, day boats to snorkel spots and outer cays, and frequent decisions shaped by rain squalls, water-taxi timing and the domestic-flight link from Panama City. It works best for travellers who enjoy improvised island days rather than polished resort predictability.

todo.name: Island boat loop
todo.desc: Use water taxis or a boat tour to connect beaches, mangroves and snorkel stops across the archipelago.

todo.name: Red Frog Beach
todo.desc: Spend a day on Isla Bastimentos for surf, jungle edges and a wilder beach feel.

todo.name: Starfish Beach
todo.desc: Visit carefully and never handle starfish; the stop is best treated as wildlife viewing, not a prop photo.

todo.name: Bocas Town evening
todo.desc: Use the stilt bars and casual restaurants for the archipelago's social centre.

todo.name: Bluff Beach surf day
todo.desc: Head out when the sea is behaving for a longer, wilder beach backed by palms and a less built-up feel than town.

todo.name: Bastimentos boat-and-trail mix
todo.desc: Combine a water taxi, a short jungle walk and a beach stop so the archipelago feels more varied than just sitting in Bocas Town.

wifi.note: Fine for messaging and light planning in Bocas Town, but island lodges, power interruptions and boat days make backup mobile data and offline maps worthwhile.

prac.fltNote: Bocas is rarely a one-ticket journey from the UK. Most trips run through Panama City, then require either a transfer from Tocumen (PTY) across town to Albrook (PAC) for the domestic Air Panama hop to BOC, or a much longer bus-and-boat route via Almirante. Same-day international to domestic connections can be fragile if the PTY-PAC transfer slips.

airports.name: Bocas del Toro Isla Colon Airport
Marcos A. Gelabert Airport, Panama City

prac.lang: Spanish; English is useful in Bocas and Panama City tourism services
prac.visa: British citizens can normally visit Panama for up to 3 months without a visa, but may be asked for an onward ticket, accommodation details and proof of funds at the border.
alerts: Rain is common even in the better months, so protect electronics and soft luggage on boat transfers.
Water taxis can be choppy after dark or in squalls, and some island logistics effectively shut down earlier than mainland travellers expect.
Respect wildlife rules around starfish, coral and mangroves; treat them as viewing zones, not handling opportunities.
If you fly in via Panama City, leave enough time for the PTY-to-PAC transfer or plan an overnight stop rather than gambling on a tight same-day connection.

sweet: January to March is the easiest window for boat days, beach time and lower transfer friction; December and early April can also work well if you want some winter-sun energy without the absolute February peak.
